sentences.text: Yes.
As mentioned it, you know, I went on the second transport.
It was maybe eight or 10 kids.
They took us out and told us to take all your belongings, you know, because you're going away for to work, you know.
So I didn't have too much, you know-- a pair of pants, a jacket, a shirt, a pair of shoes, you know.
I wrapped it up in a bundle.
My mother wrapped it up in the bundle, you know, and everything, and gave it to me.
And that's the last time-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] excuse me-- I saw my parents and my sisters.
[CRYING]
Well, he said goodbye.
And he said, I'll see you when you come back.
[CRYING] Then-- then we went to the biggest city, which was Wielun.
And we went by horse and wagon, you know.

Yeah, we were in covered wagon, you know, and went horse and wagon, and took us in a big building, which-- that building, they gathered people from other surroundings, from the small places, small towns, small villages.
And we stayed there may be a day, over night.
And then we're shipped out.
You know, in groups we're shipped out.
And I was shipped out to one of those camps--
--you know, civilian camps, labor camps.
Most, yeah-- most young people.
Yeah, yeah.
No, just males.
No, just one-- just males.
You know, they us-- maybe about seven-- eight or 10 kids, you know.
We were all males, you know.
To-- to-- to some civilian camps.
I can't recall the name, you know, of, you know, the camps, but some civilian camp.

Then I worked as-- we worked in a forest, you know-- cut down trees to make roads, expressways, you know, for the German army can go from one place to the next place and, you know, helped build expressways, you know.
And stayed in the camps.
Yeah, in that, you know, we had our civilian clothes, you know.
There was no uniforms then, just the clothes.
And we went to-- from day-- every day, six days a week, you know, we went to the camps.
And we worked.
And-- and when the camps-- you know, we used to get our ration-- you know, a piece of bread, a slice of bread, and a cup of soup, a bowl of soup.
And that was our daily food, you know.
I remember in one of the camps, we used to get a little loaf of bread-- you know, a little small loaf of bread.
And it's supposed to last for five days.
So we ate it up, you know, in one time.
And the next four days, I didn't eat.
I didn't have no bread.
Even we-- I could save a piece of bread, but what I'm going do with it?
Where I'm going to put it?
You know, somebody going to steal it.
So I might as well ate it up.
And you know, for the next four days, we didn't eat no bread-- just get a bowl of soup.
And-- and in the camp, I remember, they kept a storage.
They kept like, the beets, the carrots, you know, potatoes-- they kept in the cellar, you know, because that's [INAUDIBLE].
And they were-- had little windows.
And one time, at night, I tied my pants down with a piece of rope, you know, here-- tie them down.
And then I crawled into the basement window and I filled up my pants, you know, with potatoes, you know, carrots, you know, whatever it was.
And the guard was walking around, back and forth, with a machine gun.
You know, but I had to time him, so when he passed, you know, not to see me.
Because if he see me, you know, they kill me or they hang me, you know, for that.
Because it happens.
You know, for stealing a potato, or piece of carrot, or something, they could hang you.
And I went and crawled back in the camp.
In the middle-- in the camp, we had a stove.
You know, and I used to take out and the carrots, you know, and put in the ashes, you know.
And that's how heat it up, you know, in the shed, you know, with some guys, you know, and other things.
So that we tried to do to survive.
You steal anything, as long to survive, you know.
But that's what we did.
Well, with the-- with the people that I work, you know.
That's-- that's your friends, you know.
That's all you-- you know.
You know, is you go to work, survive, come back, and next day the same thing, you know.
And yeah, that's what we did.
And--
Yeah, yeah, could have--
Well, it happened to a friend of mine that-- from my hometown, that he stole, you know, a potato.
And they hang him.
And I don't know which camp it was, but they hang him for-- for stealing a potato.
His brother was a good friend of mine.
And that was my friend's brother.
They-- they hanged him for--
No, no, I--
50 years ago, you know, those things happens, you know.
Yeah, this is-- no, this-- those-- the camps I was was Jewish.
In fact, we had lice.
I got marks on my legs, you know, from the lice that they ate us.
You know, we used to throw at each other the lice, you know.
[LAUGHING] And once in a while that what I used to do is take us to a place where they give you a shower.
And they take-- I used to wrap up their clothes in a bundle.
And you put in, like a furnace, a hot furnace, you know, to-- like in a steam, to steam you clothes, you know, to kill the lice, you know, and everything.
And they gave you a nice-- a hot bath, you know, and to get rid of the lice.
So four weeks later, it'd be the same thing, you know.
It lasted, you know, four weeks, and then back to the same thing.
I would say in two that I can recall.
Yeah, from that one, we went to another one in Germany, another labor camp, which I worked in an ammunition factory.
Yeah.
It was-- the plant was like-- everything was out of cement and steel.
On top was growing grass, trees, like in a forest.
You couldn't tell, you know, from-- an aeroplane went by, you couldn't that it is ammunition factory.
Because it looked like, you know, nice landscaping and everything.
But it was an ammunition factory.
And I worked on a machine that I put in a powder to make some kind of cannon or whatever it was.
I don't know what it was, you know.
But the end of my fingers, you know, all the skin was gone.
Well you know, it came off, it was so hot-- the powder.
And then one time, I-- I must have put in too much powder in that machine.
And it blew up-- you know, a fire.
And over those machines was a big kettles-- tanks, big tanks of water.
You know, and in case it happened like this here, you ran outside.
And you had a level-- a level.
And you pulled that level.
And the tank tips over, and the water comes out, and, you know, distinguish the fire.
And that's what I did and everything.
And then, when I got back into the camp, they told me I did sabotage-- you know, when the officers or whatever it was, and that they're going to hang me, you know, for that.
Yeah, yeah.
And the commandant from the camp, too.
He called me.
He told me, you know, that-- you know, that they're going to hang me, because I did sabotage.
And I said, no, I didn't.
I start crying, you know.
And I begged, you know.
I said it was an accident.
You know, I didn't mean it, to do it, and everything, and I did everything possible, you know, to-- to defend myself.
And he said, well, we'll talk it over with the other officers to see what, you know, we should do with you, and this and that.
And I guess they didn't.
You know, they didn't hang me.
He said they said, you know, if it happens one more time, you know, that's what I get.
I get the rope, you know.
But at that time-- [LAUGHS] you know.
And we worked in that-- in that camp for a while.
And from there, I don't know for how long.
And I remember, too, on weekends, we used to go and work in a field.
You know, that was for Germany, in the field.
Because they were short of manpower.
You know, they didn't have people to work on their field.
They got us from the camps.
And we worked in the fields.
And for that, they finally gave us bags of potatoes.
You know, I remember load up the wagons.
You know, we should take this, take it to the camp.
So the soup wasn't too bad, you know.
We found a few pieces of potatoes in that camp.
In there I worked-- the same thing.
I don't remember how long.
And then I was shipped to Auschwitz from there.
We went by trucks, you know, open trucks, you know.
We're going to Auschwitz.
No, no.
We just said-- they said we are going to another different camp, you know.
No, I didn't know nothing in the camps.
I didn't know nothing about it.
And-- and when we came to Auschwitz, you know, before they let us in, into Auschwitz, they dropped us off.
And then we had to walk up to a German officer-- you know, dressed up in uniform.
And he looked at you, you know, and everything.
Or he told you, left, right, right or left, you know, and walk up.
And he looked at me.
And then, you know, he told me to go right, I guess, you know.
And I went into-- into Auschwitz.
And the one who went in the other side-- they went into the gas chamber, you know, to--
No, no.
They didn't ask.
Well, that time, [INAUDIBLE] maybe three or four years later-- maybe 18, you know. 17, something like that, you know.
I was-- and I was lucky.
I guess I was lucky.
I went into Auschwitz.
And then I stayed in Auschwitz not too long.
Can you tell me about the process of entering Auschwitz-- what you saw, what you heard, what you smelt?
Well, and as I said, when I came, when they dropped us off from the trucks, you know from the-- we walked up, you know, in front of the German.
And he looked at you.
And, you know, if you appealed to him, you know, he said go to the left or go to the right.
And I guess I went to the right because I went to the camp.
You know, and after he get through, after all the people, you know, were interviewed, you know, he said, OK.
Go in there.
And they picked us up.
Somebody was there.
And he took us in, you know, into the camp, into Auschwitz.
And they gave-- assigned the barracks, you know.
And the other ones went to the furnace, you know.
Did you go through a shower?
I don't remember that.
But it's possible.
I don't remember that.
But that-- and then that I got my uniform, you know.
And I got my number on my arm.
And they gave me the number, too, you know.
Yeah.
Yeah, that's from Auschwitz.
Well I was assigned to the-- to the barracks, you know, in the building.
And I stayed there-- I don't know how long.
Not too long.
Maybe a couple months, maybe three months.
And then I was shipped out to Jaworzno.
No, I went to-- where did I go to?
Birkenau.
I worked a few times in Birkenau.
Oh, like I work on the field, I remember, too.
I don't remember what, but I mean, I work in the fields-- you know, not too long.
And then-- then I was-- and then I remember, we had women guards there too.
And I did something wrong, you know.
I don't know what I did, but she came after me and slapped over my face, you know.
But I don't remember what I did, you know.
And she beat the hell out of me, you know.
Well, they were German, German women, you know, in uniform, you know.
Well, Polish-- Polish people that I know, that I remember, you know.
And then I stayed, as I said-- in Auschwitz.
Then I was shipped out to Jaworzno, the same thing, by truck.
Jaworzno was like-- I would say like a branch of Auschwitz.
And then I worked in the coal mines there.
Well, it was less people, you know, and-- but the treatment was the same thing.
You know, you get a loaf of bread-- I mean, a slice of bread, and a bowl of soup.
And over there, I remember you used to get a loaf of bread was for five people.
So every time, you know, we get a loaf of bread, it was the same five-- five people-- usually, you know.
And then you sliced the bread in five slices.
Now one might be bigger than the other one slice.
But how do you get?
Everybody-- if you see it, everybody would like to get the biggest slice.
So what we did, whoever sliced the bread, he took two pieces of bread.
He put behind his back.
And then, you know, you tell him, you want your left hand-- arm-- your left hand or your one from your right hand.
You know, this way there was no argument, whatever you pick.
That's how, you know, you made sure you got your share.
Can you describe the living conditions?
Well, it was clean.
We had to keep clean.
And the best time we had, we had an extra bowl of soup, or a thicker bowl of soup when the Red Cross come in, you know, to check on us.
Well, used to get a piece of salami, you know.
The soup was thicker.
You know, and you used to be clean.
We used to scrub the floors, you know, and everything had to be clean, you know.
And you had to be cleaned, you know.
No, no.
No, they just came in to check it.
And then they said, oh well, you know, it doesn't look too bad, you know, for prisoners.
Yeah, and then I worked in the coal mines.
And I went to-- to work-- put chains on the wrists.
You know, we were chained down to go to work.
Like, there was a big rod.
On the end of the rod was like a-- well, like a hook.
You know, when you slip your chain in, you see, you couldn't slip out.
You know, and there was maybe, I would say, 10 guys on each side that rod.
And the end of the rod was flattened down, and there was a hole in it, you see, to put a lock in, in that rod, you know with a key.
And the kapo, you know, he had-- had the key, you know.
So before you walk out from the barracks in the morning, you slip on the chain, you know, and the rod, and went to work.
We walked to work and the Germans with machine guns, you know, on the side.
And we came-- before the coal mine, you know, going down into the coal mine, they took the lock off.
You know and [INAUDIBLE].
You slip out the chain.
You gave to the kapo, you know, the chain.
And he gave it to the German, you know, whoever it is.
And we worked down-- walked down in the coal mine.
And we worked in the coals, you know, in the coal mines.
And I was help, you know-- and a Polish guy, he was like the foreman.
I mean, he worked in the coal mine, too.
But he's the one who used the ammunition, you know, where you go in-- to blow up the coals, you know, and everything.
And he got paid from the general government.
Like, he paid for the city or the state, you know, and he was-- because we couldn't use no ammunition.
You know, we used to load up the coals on conveyors, after he shoot out the coals.
And we used to load on conveyors.
And we used to load them on small wagons and push them up to-- on the top, to get on the top to-- and this-- this Polish guy, oh, he was a bastard.
You know, I can say that.
Because [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the conveyors were running on machines, you knows, motors.
And he said, do you see this hear?
And he said, [POLISH], you know, like in Polish.
You know, he told me, [POLISH].
He said, this is the oil of your father and mother, you know, that use from the machines.
[CRYING] I was just praying I hope that someday soon-- you know, something happens to him, you know, and everything.
And one day, sure enough, when we went in, in the mine, we were digging.
And there was a loose big sheet of coal on top, you know.
And he didn't check it.
And it fell down.
And then-- and hurt him-- you know, hurt his back, or whatever it was.
And then he told me-- he said, yeah, that I put a curse on him, you know-- that's why it happened, yeah.
No, no, he was Polish.
No.
And then I was transferred to a different-- guy the same thing.
You know, like he was working with a Polish guy.
And he was to me like a father.
You know, he-- he would even share a piece of bread with me, you know, that he had.
And his was a ration, too, you know.
And he would share with me.
And he was so good to me.
And the other one, and one time he tried to-- and it comes lunch time.
You know, he had lunch, you know.
You know, but we didn't have nothing.
I didn't have nothing to eat.
So he ate his lunch.
And I just sat till the half hour was over, you know.
And one time, he offered me a piece of bread, one time.
I didn't even want to take his piece of bread from the other guy, you know.
I said, no, you eat it.
I said, I don't need it.
I mean, I don't care if I die here, but I wouldn't take it, you know.
And then that came to Jaworzno.
I was a dishwasher.
Like after the people had their soup, you know, I used to wash the dishes.
Because I knew the kapo.
Somehow he start a liking to me.
And a kapo is like a foreman, you know.
And so me and another kid used to wash the dishes.
And for that, you know, I used to get an extra bowl of soup.
You know, I used to hold that big kettle.
You know, they had to dig, you know, the soup, and put in the soup.
And I used to hold the kettle.
And sometimes I bent back a little bit, the kettle.
See, this way, there was more soup left.
You know, because whatever was left in the kettle, you know, we used to share it.
And then I took that bowl of soup and trade it with somebody else for a piece of bread.
Because the other guy-- the soup would fill them up more than the piece of bread.
So they gave me a slice of bread.
So that piece of bread-- I had an extra piece of bread.
So I could take it to work, you know.
And I had-- which nobody had a piece of bread.
And I had a piece of bread.
And I used to put the piece of bread in my uniform, sleep in it, you know.
And I didn't-- because the same thing-- I had no place where to put it.
You know, so I used to-- and I had a piece of extra bread.
So you know, I had a little bit of extra food.
And that came because account of the kapo.
And then one time, he gave me, you know, to-- because, you know, we worked with Polish people in the coal mines.
He used to gave me to smuggle into the camp a package of tobacco, you know.
And if they catch you to, you know, smuggle in, they hang you.
So-- but I mean, if I-- you know, when you get an extra bowl of soup, you know, why not?
So we do it.
And so I put the tobacco between my legs-- you know, put it in my uniform, between my legs.
And when you walk in the camp, the Germans, you know, search you all over, you know.
And somehow, he didn't-- he didn't touch me there, you know, and everything.
I was just-- you know, if they catch me-- and I got through.
And I said, no more.
I didn't do it no more, just one time.
And I smuggled in.
You know, I got a bowl of soup.
In the same camp too, I saw maybe at least 16 people-- Polish people-- they hung.
They hang them.
Because they tried to dug a tunnel under the barracks, you know, and get through to the fence, and, you know escape.
Well, they were Polish people.
So you know, and we are in Poland.
You know, they wouldn't have no problem.
And somehow they were caught.
And they hanged them.
Because they used to take the dirt, what they dig out of the tunnel-- the barracks used to stay on post.
So it was empty, hollow underneath.
So the dirt, they took and put it behind, underneath the barracks, so nobody could notice it.
I mean, you could stay right here.
You didn't go and look under there, underneath, what happening.
You know, and that's how they put the dirt.
And they caught them.
They were caught.
And they hang them.
And I remember-- it was on tables-- you know, big, long tables.
You know, they put the gallows on the top, and the ropes hang down.
And-- and they put them on-- on bricks-- you know, the legs, from the tables, they put on the bricks.
And each leg had a rope, you know.
And there were the prisoners.
They selected, you know, where the prisoners held the rope on the legs, you know.
And under the command, you know, from some German officer, they pulled the tables.
You see the legs.
And you know, and that's how they hung them.
You know, and they-- you know, they pull out [INAUDIBLE], underneath-- underneath from their feet.
You know, they pulled down the tables.
Yeah.
Yeah, yeah.
They let us home from work.
We left earlier from work to witness, to see what's happening.
They put the Germans on top of the roofs with machine guns-- you know, double there the guards, triple the guards.
So because they're afraid they shouldn't-- you know, something happen, you know, something-- yeah, they let us home early for work, to witness, to see.
And they says, if that's what you're going to do again, this what's going to happen.
You know, and that's what you're going to get, the rope.
You know, and they hang about 16 guys.
Not that I know, no.
Not that I know.
Well, I slept like, on bunks, you know, a bed, you know, with straw, you know.
You had a pillow and a blanket.
You know, and slept.
It was-- at that time, it was two or three on top on each other.
I remember, I slept on the bottom, you know--
--not too far from the kapo, you know.
No, that didn't matter, you know.
And stay there and work.
You know, and I remember when I-- when I spit out or something, I spit out black.
You know, it came out black from the mines, you know.
I mean, you could stay and work right next to one another, stay with friends, you know, work.
And maybe he didn't use the shovel right, you know, put enough dirt, enough coal, you know, on the shovel.
They pulled them out, and bang bang, and shot them, you know, right there, in front.
And I figured, I-- you know, as long as I can work, I just have to work.
Maybe this will save me.
And then I worked as-- and some of the guys, you know-- like in the coal mines, you see, you get a little lamp.
I don't know what you call it, but a little lam.
You know, you go down the coal mine and it's dark.
And that's how you work by, by this little thing.
And the wood posts, you know to holding up the ceiling-- and what you did with the lamp, you know, you hit it.
You know, it had a hook.
And you put the little lamp on that hook.
And you worked by there.
You see, and that's the light you had there, right there.
And the foreman could stay a little bit, a few feet away, so you cannot see him.
But he can see you.
Because it's dark.
And if he sees, you know, some guy doesn't perform, he doesn't work the way it is, he just walked up to him and said, hey, you didn't do nothing.
Or he beat the shit out of him.
Or he shot him-- you know, killed him.
And used to get him up, you know, on stretchers.
Some guys never made it up-- you know, went down and never made it up.
Oh, I don't know.
I don't know.
Yeah.
No, no-- a few hundred in that particular, you know, Jaworzno.
And then stayed there and till the Russians start coming in, the Russian army.
What time what?
Well, I would say that was under-- well, 1945.
In the beginning of 1945.
Maybe January, you know, went and started going on the-- on the march.
Not a thing.
No, we didn't know.
Oh, we heard planes.
Yeah, we heard planes.
In fact, if I'm not mistaken, a day before or two days before, the Russian planes came in and bombed Jaworzno.
You know, they bombed the German barracks.
Yeah, yeah.
They bombed the German barracks.
They missed us, you know.
They bombed the German barracks.
Well, yeah, yeah.
Well, they're talking.
You know, we know that hte Russian is coming in, you know.
Well, we said, well, maybe we'll be liberated.
You know, maybe we're free.
Maybe we'll be able to go home.
And then they-- next couple days, they start taking us on that march, you know.
Oh, yeah.
They took us out.
And we're marching.
And I don't know how many days or how many nights.
I don't remember that.
But you know, I was getting weaker and weaker.

And on the march, when you went on a march, and somebody fell down, and he couldn't make it no more, he got tired, weak, you know, and everything, the last German in the column, you know, took out his pistol and, you know, gave you a bullet, you know, in the head, you know.
And then we took the person, with-- moved them over to the side, you know, he shouldn't lay in the middle of the road and everything.
And I hardly could make it any more.
And it comes at night-- at night, we used to rest, and mostly on a farm.
And the farmer used to cook up-- you know, boil up a potato.
And that's what you got-- a potato or a glass of water, you know.
Yeah, yeah.
Yes, you march.
You didn't get nothing.
No, you just-- when you came to the rest, you got a potato or a glass of water.
And you stayed at barn overnight.
You know, and then next day, you march again.
And we're marching.
And I couldn't make it any more.
You know, I figured, well, let me-- you know, that's it.
You know, he shoot me, he shoot me.
You know-- nothing, I can't walk.
And with the column, we walk next to guys.
And they said, no, no, no, no, he said.
Schlomo Hersch-- you know what they said?
It's getting dark.
He said, please try to make it.
You know, because we have come to our rest, you know, and everything.
And I could not hold onto them.
And they dragged me, you know.
And I walked.
And I made it, you know.
And I made it to the farm.
And I came on the farm.
And I lay down on the floor.
I couldn't make it no further.
I lay down.
And the same token-- the farmer boiled up a potato.
So I couldn't get him-- go down there and get the potato.
But the friends-- you know, picked a potato.
Gave me a potato, a glass of water.
And I made it into the barn.
Yeah, OK.
